subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to the visual world of mathematics! In this unit you will learn how graphs turn numbers and relationships into pictures. A graph can show at a glance something that a long list of numbers hides. When Amina plots the temperature in Hargeisa hour by hour, when Yusuf tracks how far a bus has travelled from Mogadishu over time, or when Halima works out the cost of buying mangoes at the Eastleigh market, they are all using the ideas in this unit.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Plot and read points in all four quadrants using ordered pairs (x, y).
Name the axes, the origin, and the four quadrants correctly.
Understand a linear function and the meaning of y = mx + c.
Calculate a gradient (slope) as a rate of change and find a y-intercept.
Draw a straight-line graph using a table of values and using gradient and intercept.
Read values from a graph, including interpolation and extrapolation.
Find the point where two lines intersect and explain what it means.
Interpret real-life graphs such as distance–time and cost graphs.

concepts.title: The Coordinate Plane
concepts.explanation: The grid we use for graphing is called the coordinate plane, or the Cartesian plane after the French mathematician René Descartes. It is built from two number lines that cross at right angles. The horizontal line is the x-axis; positive numbers run to the right and negative numbers to the left. The vertical line is the y-axis; positive numbers run upward and negative numbers downward. The point where they cross is the origin, written (0, 0). To plot the point (3, 2), start at the origin, move 3 units right along…
concepts.example: The two axes cut the plane into four regions called quadrants, numbered anticlockwise starting from the top right:

concepts.title: Functions and Relationships
concepts.explanation: A function is a rule that links two quantities so that each input gives exactly one output. Think of a function as a machine: you put a number in, the machine follows its rule, and one number comes out. If the rule is “multiply by 2, then add 1”, then putting in 3 gives 2×3 + 1 = 7. We can write this rule as an equation, y = 2x + 1, where x is the input and y is the output. The input is called the independent variable because you are free to choose it, and the output is called the dependent variable because its…
concepts.example: One relationship can be shown in several matching ways, and a strong mathematician can move between them:

concepts.title: Linear Functions and y = mx + c
concepts.explanation: A linear function is one whose graph is a perfectly straight line. Every linear function can be written in the form y = mx + c, where m is the gradient (how steep the line is) and c is the y-intercept (where the line crosses the y-axis). These two numbers tell you everything you need to draw the line, so it is worth understanding each one carefully. The sign of the gradient tells you the direction of the line:
concepts.example: Positive gradient (m > 0): the line goes up from left to right.

concepts.title: Drawing Straight-Line Graphs
concepts.explanation: There are two reliable methods for drawing the graph of a linear function. Learn both, because each is quicker in different situations. Choose three or four x-values, work out each matching y-value, plot the points, and join them with a ruled straight line. Three points is enough, and the third acts as a check: if the three do not lie in a straight line, one of them is wrong. For y = 2x − 1 we might choose x = 0, 1, 2 and 3, giving y = −1, 1, 3 and 5. That produces the points (0, −1), (1, 1), (2, 3) and (3, 5).…
concepts.example: Always label your axes with the variable and any units, choose a sensible scale so the line fills the grid, and extend the line a little beyond your points with arrows to show it continues. A graph without labels is…

concepts.title: Reading and Interpreting Graphs
concepts.explanation: Drawing a graph is only half the skill; the other half is reading one that is already drawn. Graphs answer questions quickly once you know how to look. Direct reading finds a value straight from the line. To answer “what is y when x = 4?”, go up from 4 on the x-axis until you meet the line, then across to the y-axis to read the value. To answer “when is y = 10?”, do the reverse: start at 10 on the y-axis, go across to the line, then down to the x-axis.
concepts.example: Interpolation means reading a value between points you already know – for example estimating the temperature at 2:30 pm when you have readings at 2:00 and 3:00. Because the values sit inside the data, interpolation is…

concepts.title: Real-Life Graphs
concepts.explanation: The reason graphs matter is that they describe the real world. Many everyday situations follow a linear rule, and putting them on a graph makes the story easy to read. Distance–time graphs show a journey. Time goes on the x-axis and distance on the y-axis. A straight sloping line means steady speed; the steeper the line, the faster the travel. A flat, horizontal line means a stop – time passes but distance does not change. Suppose Hassan cycles from his home towards the masjid at a steady 12 km/h. After t hours he…
concepts.example: Cost graphs often have a fixed charge plus a rate. A taxi in Nairobi might charge a flat 200 shillings plus 100 shillings per kilometre. The cost of a journey of d kilometres is C = 100d + 200. Here the y-intercept 200…

methods.title: How to draw a line from y = mx + c
methods.example: In which quadrant does the point (−3, 5) lie?
steps: Plot the y-intercept c on the y-axis.
From there, use the gradient m: go 1 right and m up (or down if m is negative).
Mark a second and third point the same way.
Rule a straight line through the points and label it.

methods.title: How to find where a line crosses each axis
methods.example: Find the gradient of the line through (2, 3) and (6, 11).
steps: For the y-intercept, set x = 0 and work out y.
For the x-intercept, set y = 0 and solve for x.
Plot both points and rule the line through them.

methods.title: How to find where two lines meet
methods.example: State the gradient and y-intercept of y = −2x + 7.
steps: Set the two right-hand sides equal to each other.
Solve the equation to find x.
Put x back into either equation to find y.
Write the intersection as the point (x, y) and check it in the other equation.

methods.title: Build a table of values
methods.example: Complete a table for y = 3x − 1 using x = −1, 0, 1, 2.
steps: Solution: When x = −1, y = 3(−1) − 1 = −4.
When x = 0, y = −1.
When x = 1, y = 2.
When x = 2, y = 5.

methods.title: Find an x-intercept
methods.example: Where does the line y = 2x − 6 cross the x-axis?
steps: Solution: On the x-axis, y = 0.
So 0 = 2x − 6, giving 2x = 6 and x = 3.
The line crosses the x-axis at the point (3, 0).

methods.title: Find where two lines meet
methods.example: Find the intersection of y = 2x + 1 and y = −x + 7.
steps: Solution: At the crossing point both y-values are equal, so 2x + 1 = −x + 7.
Add x to both sides: 3x + 1 = 7.
Subtract 1: 3x = 6, so x = 2.

workedExamples.title: Name the quadrant
workedExamples.prompt: In which quadrant does the point (−3, 5) lie?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The x-coordinate −3 is negative (left) and the y-coordinate 5 is positive (up). Left and up is the top-left region, which is Quadrant II.

workedExamples.title: Calculate a gradient
workedExamples.prompt: Find the gradient of the line through (2, 3) and (6, 11).
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Gradient = change in y ÷ change in x = (11 − 3) ÷ (6 − 2) = 8 ÷ 4 = 2. The line rises 2 units for every 1 unit across.

workedExamples.title: Read m and c
workedExamples.prompt: State the gradient and y-intercept of y = −2x + 7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The number in front of x is the gradient, so m = −2 (a downward slope). The constant term is the y-intercept, so c = 7, meaning the line crosses the y-axis at (0, 7).

workedExamples.title: Build a table of values
workedExamples.prompt: Complete a table for y = 3x − 1 using x = −1, 0, 1, 2.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: When x = −1, y = 3(−1) − 1 = −4. When x = 0, y = −1. When x = 1, y = 2. When x = 2, y = 5. The points are (−1, −4), (0, −1), (1, 2) and (2, 5). Plotted, they lie in a straight line.

workedExamples.title: Find an x-intercept
workedExamples.prompt: Where does the line y = 2x − 6 cross the x-axis?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On the x-axis, y = 0. So 0 = 2x − 6, giving 2x = 6 and x = 3. The line crosses the x-axis at the point (3, 0).

workedExamples.title: Find where two lines meet
workedExamples.prompt: Find the intersection of y = 2x + 1 and y = −x + 7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: At the crossing point both y-values are equal, so 2x + 1 = −x + 7. Add x to both sides: 3x + 1 = 7. Subtract 1: 3x = 6, so x = 2. Then y = 2(2) + 1 = 5. The lines meet at (2, 5). Check: −x + 7 = −2 + 7 = 5. ✓

workedExamples.title: Interpret a real-life graph
workedExamples.prompt: A taxi fare is C = 100d + 200 (shillings, for d kilometres). Find the fare for 6 km, and explain the numbers 100 and 200.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: C = 100(6) + 200 = 600 + 200 = 800 shillings. The 200 is the fixed charge before the taxi moves (the y-intercept). The 100 is the cost of each kilometre (the gradient).

workedExamples.title: Read a distance–time graph
workedExamples.prompt: On a distance–time graph, a line rises steadily from (0, 0) to (2, 24), where distance is in km and time in hours. What is the speed?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Speed is the gradient = change in distance ÷ change in time = 24 ÷ 2 = 12 km/h. The line is straight, so the speed is constant throughout.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Write the equation of the line with gradient 3 that crosses the y-axis at (0, −2).

workedExamples.title: State whether each pair of lines is parallel
workedExamples.prompt: State whether each pair of lines is parallel: a) y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x − 5 b) y = 3x and y = −3x.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: On a distance–time graph, describe what each part means: a) a steep upward line b) a flat horizontal line.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find where y = 3x − 1 and y = x + 5 intersect, and check your answer by substitution.

activities.title: Make a Giant Coordinate Grid
activities.materials: a large sheet of paper or chalk on the ground, a ruler, and small stones as markers.
steps: Draw two number lines crossing at right angles to make x- and y-axes, numbered from −5 to 5.
Call out coordinates like (3, −2) and place a stone at each point.
Ask a partner to name the quadrant of every stone you place.
Join four stones to make a square or rectangle, then read off all four corner coordinates.
Keep your grid – you can use it for the plotting games below.

activities.title: Coordinate Picture Puzzle
steps: Choose a simple shape – a house, a boat, or a star.
Draw it on the grid and write down the coordinates of each corner in order.
Give only the list of coordinates to a friend and let them plot and join the points.
Compare their picture with your original. Did the coordinates describe it exactly?

activities.title: The Gradient Walk
activities.materials: an outdoor space and a way to measure steps.
steps: Walk at a steady pace and have a partner call out the time every 10 seconds.
Count how many steps you have taken at each call and record the pairs (time, steps).
Plot the points on squared paper – they should lie roughly on a straight line.
Work out the gradient (steps per second). That gradient is your walking speed!
Try walking faster and slower and see how the steepness of the line changes.

activities.title: Taxi Fare Investigation
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a made-up fare rule.
steps: Invent a taxi rule, for example a 200-shilling fixed charge plus 100 shillings per kilometre.
Make a table of the fare for 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 kilometres.
Plot the points and rule a straight line through them.
Use your graph to answer: what does a 3.5 km trip cost, and how far can you travel for 700 shillings?

activities.title: Line-Up Match Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: cards with equations on some and gradients/intercepts on others.
steps: Write equation cards like y = 2x + 1, y = −x + 4, y = 3x.
Write matching fact cards like ‘gradient 2, intercept 1’.
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down.
Take turns flipping two cards; keep a pair if the equation matches its facts. Most pairs wins.

activities.title: Journey Story Graphs
steps: Think of a real journey, such as cycling to the masjid, resting, then cycling home.
Sketch a distance–time graph that tells this story, using sloped lines for movement and flat lines for stops.
Write two sentences describing what happens in each part of your graph.
Swap graphs with a friend and try to tell each other's journey story from the graph alone.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand gradient best? What was the trickiest part of plotting or reading a graph? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: A function is a rule that links
rules.text: A function is a rule that links two quantities so that each input gives exactly one output. Think of a function as a machine: you put a number in, the machine follows its rule, and one number comes out. If the rule is “multiply by 2, then add 1”, then putting in 3 gives 2×3 + 1 = 7. We can write this rule as an equation, y = 2x + 1, where x is the input and y is the output.

rules.title: A table of values is the bridge
rules.text: A table of values is the bridge between an equation and a graph. To build one, choose some x-values, put each into the rule, and record the y-value it produces. For y = 2x + 1: when x = 0, y = 1; when x = 1, y = 3; when x = 2, y = 5. This gives the points (0, 1), (1, 3) and (2, 5), which we can then plot. Each point is a single example of the rule; the full graph shows *every* example at once. That is the quiet power of a graph – it holds an endless list of number pairs in one picture.

rules.title: Choose three or four x-values, work out
rules.text: Choose three or four x-values, work out each matching y-value, plot the points, and join them with a ruled straight line. Three points is enough, and the third acts as a check: if the three do not lie in a straight line, one of them is wrong. For y = 2x − 1 we might choose x = 0, 1, 2 and 3, giving y = −1, 1, 3 and 5. That produces the points (0, −1), (1, 1), (2, 3) and (3, 5). Plot them and rule a line through all four.

rules.title: Read m and c straight from the
rules.text: Read m and c straight from the equation. Plot the y-intercept first, then use the gradient to step to more points. For y = 2x − 1: plot the intercept at (0, −1). The gradient is 2 = 2/1, so from (0, −1) move 1 right and 2 up to reach (1, 1); repeat to reach (2, 3). Rule the line. For a negative gradient such as −3, you step 1 right and 3 down each time.

rules.title: A third handy idea is to use
rules.text: A third handy idea is to use the two intercepts. Find the y-intercept by setting x = 0, and the x-intercept by setting y = 0, then rule a line through both. For y = 2x − 6, setting x = 0 gives (0, −6) and setting y = 0 gives 0 = 2x − 6, so x = 3 and the point (3, 0). Two well-separated points are all you need for a straight line.

rules.title: The reason graphs matter is that they
rules.text: The reason graphs matter is that they describe the real world. Many everyday situations follow a linear rule, and putting them on a graph makes the story easy to read.

terms: An ordered pair (x, y) giving a position
The point (0, 0) where the axes cross
One of the four regions of the plane
Steepness: change in y over change in x
Where a line crosses the y-axis (x = 0)
Where a line crosses the x-axis (y = 0)
A rule whose graph is a straight line
commonMistakes: Gradient is change in y over change in x
y-intercept of y = 3x − 5 said to be 3
Always use a ruler and plot carefully

realProblems.prompt: A market trader in Kismaayo sells bariis (rice) at 80 shillings per scoop, with a fixed sack charge of 100 shillings. Write the cost rule C for s scoops, and find the cost of 6 scoops.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim walks to the masjid at a steady speed, covering 3 km in 40 minutes. What is his speed in km per hour, and how long would 4.5 km take at the same pace?
realProblems.answer: 3 km in 40 min = 4.5 km/h; 4.5 km at 4.5 km/h takes 1 hour (60 min).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 km in 40 min = 4.5 km/h; 4.5 km at 4.5 km/h takes 1 hour (60 min).

realProblems.prompt: A tank in Berbera holds 300 litres and fills at 20 litres per minute. Write the rule for litres L after t minutes, and find when it holds 500 litres.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A conversion line changes kilometres to miles with roughly M = 0.6K. Use it to convert 20 km to miles, and 12 miles back to kilometres (to the nearest whole number).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Two phone plans: Plan A costs 100 + 5t shillings and Plan B costs 40 + 8t shillings for t messages. For how many messages do the plans cost the same, and what is that cost?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find where y = 3x − 1 and y = x + 5 intersect, and check your answer by substitution.
A line passes through (2, 7) and (5, 16). Find its gradient, then its equation in the form y = mx + c.
Sagal says, ‘A line with a bigger number in front of x is always steeper.’ Is she right? Explain with an example comparing y = 2x and y = −5x.
The line y = mx + c passes through (0, 4) and (3, 0). Find m and c.
Explain the key idea in Reading and Interpreting Graphs.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Drawing a graph is only half the skill; the other half is reading one that is already drawn. Graphs answer questions quickly once you know how to look. Direct reading finds a value straight from the line. To answer “what is y when x = 4?”, go up from 4 on the x-axis until you meet the line, then across to the y-axis to read the value. To answer “when is y = 10?”, do the reverse: start at 10 on the y-axis, go across to the line, then down to the x-axis.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Real-Life Graphs.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The reason graphs matter is that they describe the real world. Many everyday situations follow a linear rule, and putting them on a graph makes the story easy to read. Distance–time graphs show a journey. Time goes on the x-axis and distance on the y-axis. A straight sloping line means steady speed; the steeper the line, the faster the travel. A flat, horizontal line means a stop – time passes but distance does not change. Suppose Hassan cycles from his home towards the masjid at a steady 12 km/h. After t hours he…

selfAssessment: I can plot and read points in all four quadrants using ordered pairs (x, y).
I can name the axes, the origin, and the four quadrants correctly.
I can understand a linear function and the meaning of y = mx + c.
I can calculate a gradient (slope) as a rate of change and find a y-intercept.
I can draw a straight-line graph using a table of values and using gradient and intercept.
I can read values from a graph, including interpolation and extrapolation.
I can find the point where two lines intersect and explain what it means.
I can interpret real-life graphs such as distance–time and cost graphs.
